coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nanodiamonds using liquid-phase laser ablation of graphene and its application in resistive random access memory
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Thomas, Anna
Parvathy, M.S.
Jinesh, K.B.
coredata.dc:description: Nanodiamonds (ND) are carbon particles with a size less than 100 nm, formed by sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms. They have attracted considerable research interest for their potential applications ranging from sensing and bioimaging to quantum computation due to its physical, chemical and surface characteristics. In this work, we report the synthesis of ND from graphene dispersions by liquid-phase laser ablation technique, using an Nd: YAG laser. ND samples exhibit a characteristic photo-absorption at 226 nm, and it shows an excitation dependent emission similar to the carbon dots. Employing Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy, the bandgap of ND is calculated to be 5.2 eV, and the work-function is around 4.2 eV. Further, we fabricated Resistive switching devices with ND film as the active layer, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) as the bottom and silver (Ag) as the top electrodes. These FTO/ND/Ag devices show excellent memory characteristics with an on-off ratio of 104 and reproducible cycling characteristics, demonstrating its potential for high-density memory applications.

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nanodiamond memristive device fabrication and characterization
Conduction mechanism of the nanodiamond RE-RAM device

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Laser ablation set up, where the graphene dispersion is kept in a crucible on a magnetic stirrer; the green arrow represents the laser beam; (b) the original graphene dispersion (dark) and the graphene dispersion after 25 minutes of laser exposure (yellow).

(a) Absorption spectra of graphene dispersion before and after laser exposure. (b) TEM images of the laser-ablated graphene dispersion sample, showing the diamond formation; (c) Raman spectra of the graphene dispersion before and after laser irradiation. Peaks correspond to nanodiamonds are marked in the spectrum. Inset shows the zoomed image of the nanodiamond peak. (d) FT-IR spectrum of graphene dispersion after laser irradiation.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (a) The elemental broad-scan of nanodiamonds. (b) the C 1s spectra of the laser exposed samples, showing clear sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms. (c) The O 1s spectra of the laser exposed samples.

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of nanodiamond solution at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300-600nm. (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra of the nanodiamond solution. Inset shows the nanodiamond solution under normal light and UV light.

(a) Scanning tunnelling microscopy images of single nanodiamond; (b) current-voltage plot obtained for a single nanodiamond; (c) I-z spectrum of nanodiamonds, from which its work function can be estimated. (d) The obtained schematic band diagram of ND from the STS analysis. Ec and Ev represent the conduction band minimum and the valence band maximum. Eg is the bandgap energy of the ND, EF and Ei represents the Fermi level and the intrinsic level respectively.

(a) Schematic representation of the FTO/ND/Ag ReRAM device. (b) A semi-logarithmic plot of the I-V characteristics of a typical ReRAM device with a SET and RESET voltage of −2.3 V and 3.8 V respectively. (c) The Weibull distributions for SET and RESET voltages of the devices (d) Retention measurement of the FTO/ND/Ag device measured at −0.8 V. (e) cycling measurement in a sequence of write- read-erase-read with voltage pulses of −3 V, 0.5 V, 4V and 0.5 V respectively. The inset shows the schematic diagram of the applied input voltage. (f) The log-log plot of I-V characteristic for understanding the space charge limited conduction mechanism in ND ReRAM devices.

Schematic band diagram of our ReRAM device (a) Band diagram of the different layers before they make into a device and the respective work functions are denoted as ϕ (b)Band diagram of the device under thermal equilibrium condition. The bending of the band is due to the ohmic contact between the electrodes and the nanodiamond.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nanodiamonds using liquid-phase laser ablation of graphene and its application in resistive random access memory

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Space-Science and Technology (IIST), Valiamala, 695547, Thiruvananthapuram, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Space-Science and Technology (IIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Space-Science and Technology (IIST), Valiamala, 695547, Thiruvananthapuram, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanodiamonds (ND) are carbon particles with a size less than 100 nm, formed by sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms. They have attracted considerable research interest for their potential applications ranging from sensing and bioimaging to quantum computation due to its physical, chemical and surface characteristics. In this work, we report the synthesis of ND from graphene dispersions by liquid-phase laser ablation technique, using an Nd: YAG laser. ND samples exhibit a characteristic photo-absorption at 226 nm, and it shows an excitation dependent emission similar to the carbon dots. Employing Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy, the bandgap of ND is calculated to be 5.2 eV, and the work-function is around 4.2 eV. Further, we fabricated Resistive switching devices with ND film as the active layer, fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) as the bottom and silver (Ag) as the top electrodes. These FTO/ND/Ag devices show excellent memory characteristics with an on-off ratio of 104 and reproducible cycling characteristics, demonstrating its potential for high-density memory applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanodiamond (ND) is a nanostructured allotrope of carbon, which has found potential applications due to its mechanical, electronics and optoelectronic properties. ND has various applications in coatings and as lubricant additives due to its surface properties [1,2]. Further, because of its low toxicity, chemical stability, and inert core with functionalized surfaces, ND and their derivatives are promising candidates for biomedical applications, especially as drug delivery systems [3]. Recent reports show that the nitrogen-vacancy centre or colour centre present in the ND formed during its synthesis process can be employed for the quantum information processing [4,5]. However, the synthesis techniques of ND followed so far involves either a large mechanical or thermal budget. Examples of such methods are detonation technique [6], high energy ball milling of micro-diamond crystals (HPHT) [7], chemical vapour deposition [8]etc. Detonation method is the most common method for synthesising ND in large scale [9], and it is carried out by detonating mixture of carbon sources such as TNT and hexogen under a metallic chamber in an atmosphere of N2 and CO2. The resultant soot is collected and purified to get the ND. However, this route to synthesis ND is expensive, as it involves extreme synthesis conditions and requires painstaking purification processes to get ultra-pure ND. Therefore, an alternative synthesis method is required for the low-cost, high yield production of ND. An efficient method that has been proposed recently is liquid-phase pulsed laser ablation, in which a pulsed laser ablates a target material in a liquid medium or dispersed solution. Pulsed laser ablation provides an opportunity to synthesize ND from different media such as the liquid-solid interface of a graphite target immersed in water [10]), solid-liquid dispersion [11], solution [12] etc.

In addition to its exciting possibilities in biomedical applications and for hard-coatings, ND can be excellent candidates for resistive memory applications as we demonstrated further in this work. Resistive random access memory (ReRAM), a forthcoming memory technology that has a high potential for scaling down compared to the FLASH technologies and has remarkable features such as high operating speed, low power consumption, simplicity in fabrication and potential ability for 3D integration etc. [13,14]. It is working based on the resistive switching phenomenon, and it is realized through the switching between high resistance state (HRS) and low resistance state (LRS). Resistive switching phenomenon has been observed in various materials such as transition metal oxides [14,15], perovskite materials [16], organic materials [17], chalcogenide materials [18,19], amorphous silicon etc. Recently resistive switching behaviour has been demonstrated for carbon-based materials [20–22]. Resistive switching of carbon nanostructure embedded systems have been reported earlier in literature. Multistage switching in Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester-based nanocomposite with high retention time(105) and the on-off ratio [23,24], random number generator based on carbon nanotube embedded threshold switching devices [25] are the few examples of such systems. Carbon-based materials have the advantages of endurances when scaled down, low cost and are compatible with the CMOS technology [20].

In this work, we report a simple one-step method to synthesize ND by the irradiation of graphene dispersions by a nanosecond Nd-YAG laser. We show that the transition of carbon sp2 hybridization to sp3 hybridization is a thermodynamically dominated process since it is a transition independent of the wavelength of the irradiation. We have measured the local density of states (LDOS) of ND using a scanning tunnelling microscope (STM) to estimate its average bandgap, which was confirmed with the absorption spectrometric measurements. Further, we fabricated resistive random access memory (ReRAM) devices using ND as the active layer, which exhibits highly reliable bipolar resistive switching characteristics.

The nanodiamond samples were synthesized by liquid-phase laser ablation of graphene dispersions. Graphene powder (Sigma Aldrich) was dispersed in isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and sonicated for one hour. The concentration of the dispersion was 0.5 mg of graphene/ml of IPA. The resultant solution was black in colour. Subsequently, this graphene dispersion was irradiated using a 532 nm Nd-YAG laser (Quanta Ray), with 8 ns pulse-width and 10 Hz repetition rate, at 2 W power. The samples were exposed to laser for 25 min until the entire solution is clear. The solution was magnetically stirred during the laser exposure to avoid gravitational settling at the bottom. The setup is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The appearance of the dispersion changed from black to pale yellow colour upon laser irradiation, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The laser-ablated solution was then filtered and used for further analysis and device fabrication.

The ND film was fabricated on fluorine-doped tin oxide substrate (FTO) by spray coating at the substrate temperature of 70 °C. After each spray cycle, the films were annealed at the same temperature for 2 min. The resultant film was around 200 nm. Further, silver (Ag) was thermally evaporated on the ND film through a shadow mask to create the top electrodes.

The bandgap of the ND was estimated using UV-Visible absorption spectrometer Cary 100 Bio UV spectrometer. Raman spectra of the samples were studied using Raman spectrometer HORIBA Scientific-Jobin Yvon Technology and the fluorescence measurements were recorded using a photoluminescence spectrometer Horiba Scientific Fluoro Max -4 spectrometer. Transmission electron micrograph was taken using a JEOL JEM 2100 high-resolution electron microscope, which has a maximum acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The STM imaging and measurements were done in nanoREV STM by Quazar Technology in constant current mode. The XPS measurement has been carried out using Thermo VG Scientific ESCALab 250. The electrical characterization of ND memory devices was performed using Agilent B2900A source measurement unit.

The apparent colour change of the solution from black to pale yellow by laser exposure indicates a drastic change either in the flake-size or electronic arrangement of carbon atoms in the original dispersion. Therefore, UV-Visible absorption spectra of the samples before and after the laser exposure was taken, and are shown in Fig. 2 (a). As compared to the sample before laser ablation, the ablated sample shows strong absorption peaks between 200 nm and 300 nm. No other peak was observed in the visible region. The absorption spectrum in Fig. 2 (a) resembles a previously reported spectrum, in which multiple peaks have been observed between 216–297 nm for the ND prepared by laser ablation of monocrystalline diamond and detonated diamond [23]. These peaks are attributed to different transitions, and the polyaromatic chromophores present in the sample [26]. Similarly, the absorption peak at 226 nm corresponds to the intrinsic absorption of the diamond (
σ →
σ *
transition) [27]. And the respective band gap has caculated as around 5.3 eV (See supporting data S1). The peaks at 200 nm and 252 nm are attributed to
π →
π
*
transition of C=C and peaks around 275 nm–300 nm corresponds to
n →
π *
transition of C=O respectively [27–29]. The peak at 239 nm is likely to be originated from multiple polyaromatic chromophores present in the sample [26]. The origin of the peak corresponding to 216 nm has been assigned to the band-band transition in ND blue-shifted due to quantum confinement effects [24]. Thus, the UV- VIS spectrum provides a clear indication of the formation of the ND. To visually confirm the transition of graphene into ND, Transmission electron micrographs have been taken for the laser-ablated graphene dispersion, and are shown in Fig. 2 (b). The clustered crystalline structure is attributed to the agglomerated ND. The average diameter of the NDs is approximately 20 nm.

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool for the structural identification of carbon allotropes. Raman spectrum of carbon has three significant bands namely T band, G band and D band, in which G and D band appeared around 1560, 1360 cm−1 respectively, under excitation of the visible wavelength and the T band will appear around 1060 cm−1 under UV excitation [30,31]. The G and D peaks are due to sp2 cites, and T peak is due to C-C sp3 cites [31]. We recorded the Raman spectrum of the samples with 532 nm laser excitation. The spectrum obtained for graphene dispersion is shown together with a sample with 25 min of laser exposure in Fig. 2(c). We could observe that the Raman peak indicating the formation of ND appears in samples after laser exposure. The observed Raman peak around 1327 cm−1 is consistent with earlier reports for detonated nanodiamonds [9]. This gives strong evidence for the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon. The D and G peaks are observed around 1345 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. It is to be noted that the 2D peak of graphene has been disappeared after laser-ablation. It indicates that the ordered graphene sheets have been converted into ND.

To study the surface chemistry of laser-ablated graphene dispersion, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed, and the results are shown in Fig. 2(d). The peaks at 3294 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 in Fig. 2(d) correspond to stretching vibrations of -OH bonding [27] and the peaks 1100–1140 cm−1 represents C-O-C asymmetric bending [27]. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1 represents the C=O stretching vibrations, and the peak at 964 cm−1 represents C-C-C bending [27]. Importantly, the peaks at 1456 cm−1,2851 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1 represent the C sp3-H bond vibrations [27]. From these observations, we can conclude that ND is decorated with different functional groups.

For further confirmation of the formation of nanodiamond, the samples were subjected to X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). XPS is particularly useful since it is possible to extract the ratio of sp3 to sp2 hybridized carbon, from the components of the C 1s spectrum [32,33]. In carbon materials, the inelastic mean free path of C 1s photoelectrons is approximately 3 nm, and it facilitates probing the whole volume of the nanoparticle [33]. The sample for the measurement was prepared by spin-coating the nanodiamond dispersion on to a clean silicon wafer. Fig. 3 (a) shows the XPS broad-scan analysis of the laser-irradiated samples. The prominent peaks observed were for O 1s and C 1s. The C 1s signal of the nanodiamonds consists of three different peaks at 284.8 eV, 286 eV and 288.7 eV corresponds to the sp3C, hydrogen and oxygen functional groups including C-H, C-OH, C-O-C and C=O respectively [34,35]. Similarly, the O1s spectrum comprises three different peaks at 531.6, 532.4 eV and 533.3 eV, which represents C sp2=O, C=O and C sp3-OH respectively, and is consistent with previous reports [36].

Nanodiamonds also show excitation dependent photoluminescence similar to carbon dots and graphene dots [37,38]. The photoluminescence spectrum of ND dispersion is shown in Fig. 4 . The solution was excited with wavelength ranging from 300–600 nm. Maximum emission occurs around 500nm excitation, which implies that the emission is from the edge states of the nanodiamonds since the large diamond bandgap is transparent to almost all the short UV to the visible wavelength [26]. Nanodiamond excitation dependent emission can be due to the interaction between the carbonyl groups and hydroxyl groups (n (OH)
→
π *
(CO)), which has been reported earlier [26,39].

For the understanding of fluorescence decay dynamics of the excited states in the nanodiamond, time-resolved lifetime measurements have been performed. These results are given in the supporting information. Figure S2 shows the decay count of photons corresponds to different emissions such as 476 nm, 530 nm and 623 nm with excitation 344 nm, 451 nm and 611 nm, respectively. The lifetime has been retrieved by fitting a double-exponential with the experimental data [40]. The two-component lifetimes correspond to different excitations are shown in table 1 in the supporting information. The extracted average lifetime of the excited states of the nanodiamonds is found to be approximately 5 ns,3ns and 0.1 ns under excitation of 344 nm, 451 nm and 611 nm respectively. It is observed that the lifetime is decreasing with an increase in the excitation, and it is consistent with the lifetime of carbon nanodots [41].

We employed scanning tunnelling microscopy (STM) as a potential method to estimate the material parameters. In addition to the topographic images, STS (Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy) measurements provide the idea of the local density of states (LDOS) of the materials, and we can study the electronic properties locally [42]. The analysis was done with an initial bias condition of −0.5 V and Tunnelling current of 0.5 nA. A typical STM topographic image of ND on the HOPG surface is shown in Fig. 6(a). The speckle kind of pattern represents the atomic features of the nanodiamond vaguely. The darker colour contrast compared to the surface (HOPG) indicates the less conductivity of ND as compared to HOPG. For the local investigation of conduction and valance band states, the dI/dV spectra have been taken. A typical local I-V plot is shown in Fig 6(b). The current corresponding to the positive sample bias is proportional to the density of states in the conduction band, and the current corresponds to the negative sample bias is proportional to the density of states of the valence band. From the dI/dV spectrum, we can observe that the Fermi level (EF) is close to the conduction band, indicating the n-type characteristics of nanodiamonds as has been reported earlier [43]. The average bandgap of the selected ND calculated from dI/dV spectra is around 4 eV.

The local work function was calculated from I-z (z is the tip-sample distance) measurement. The tunnelling current exponentially decays with increasing tunnel gap (the distance between tip and sample) as given in Eq. (1). From the slope of the I-z spectra, we can extract the workfunction of the ND using Eq. (2). The extracted workfunction is related to the tip and sample work functions and the applied bias as given by Eq. (3) [44], where
ϕ s
is the sample workfunction,
ϕ t
is the tip work function and V is the applied bias across the tip and the sample.
(1)
I =
I 0
exp
(
( − 2 )
2 m ϕ
ℏ
2
z
)
(2)
ϕ =
h 2
8
m *
π 2
[
d
(
ln
I z
)
d z
]
2
(3)
ϕ = (
ϕ s
+
ϕ t
+ e V ) / 2

Fig. 5 (c) shows a typical I-z spectrum of nanodiamonds. The average work function of the nanodiamond estimated using the above method is around5.2 eV. The obtained value is in good agreement with the previous report for ND [45]. From the information got from the STS analysis(I-z spectra and I-V measurements) for a particular ND, the schematic band diagram has been plotted as shown in Fig. 5 (d). Ec and Ev represent the bottom of the conduction band and top of the valence band. Eg is the bandgap energy of the ND, EF and Ei represents the Fermi level and the intrinsic level respectively. The discrepancy in the value of bandgap obtained from STS analysis and the absorption spectrum can be due to the band tailing resulting from the presence of surface states. At the same time, the absorption spectrum what we are getting is from a large particle distribution in the solution, which results in average absorption spectra among all the ND.

Several questions arise regarding the conversion of sp2 to sp3 phase on exposure to the intense laser beam.L. Basso et.al [10] have done detailed thermodynamic modelling of nanodiamond formation. When the laser beam is focused on the liquid, the intense electric field of the focussed laser beam generates a laser-induced plasma of the irradiated materials [10,46]. In our case, by irradiating the pulsed laser beam on graphene solution, liquid nanodroplets are formed due to the rapid increase in temperature. After each pulse, the temperature of the plume starts to decrease because of the liquid environment, and consequently, the liquid carbon rapidly return to its solid-state. The solidification depends on the cooling rate that is reported to be 1010-1011 K/s for a nanosecond pulsed laser [10]. When the carbon atom enters this supercooling regime, the diamond phase is formed as a metastable phase, which allows the nucleation of nanodiamonds, were the liquid carbon nanoclusters acts as nucleation seeds.

Generally, the evaporation rate of the target material depends on the laser parameters, the absorption efficiency of the material and the ambience. For studying the wavelength dependence of nanodiamond formation, a comparison has been done with samples prepared by second(532 nm) and third (355 nm) harmonic wavelength of Nd- YAG laser with same 200 mJ energy and constant time of 25 min. Both the samples resulted in the same yield of nano-diamonds, as shown in the Supporting data (S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanodiamond memristive device fabrication and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic representation of FTO/ND/Ag device is shown in Fig. 6 (a). The FTO/ND/Ag device was subjected to a DC bias sweeping in a sequence of 0V→-5V →0V→4V→0V. The resultant output I-V characteristics are shown in Fig. 6(b). The arrow 1 shows the initial sweep (0 to −5 V) in which the current increases suddenly when the voltage increases to a threshold value (VSET) of~ −2.3 V indicating that the resistance decreases from the high resistance state (HRS or OFF) to low resistance state (LRS or ON). This is the SET process of the device. The LRS state is maintained throughout the voltage sweep of -5 V to 0 V depicted by arrow 2. Subsequently, when we apply a voltage sweep from 0 to 4V (see arrow 3) at a certain voltage around 3.8 V(VRESET) an opposite operation was observed, marking the RESET process. With the RESET, the device retrieves its initial state represented by arrow 4. To show the repeatability, multiple resistive switching has been done and is shown in supporting data (S4).

To study the switching reliability of the ND devices, the SET and RESET voltage is plotted as Weibull distributions. The cumulative distribution function is given by
F
( V )
= 1 − e x p
(
−
V β
)
Where β is the shape parameter of the distribution, V is the SET or RESET switching voltages [47]. For representation, we have plotted
l n (
− l n (
1 − F ( V )
)
)
versus SET or RESET voltages as the distribution shown in Fig. 6(c). The average VSET and VRESET are found to be -1.79±0.35 and 4.16±0.72, respectively. The SET voltage distribution has a single slope with β~3.4, indicating a reliable switching. At the same time, the distribution corresponds to the RESET voltage have two distinct slopes 5.47 and 1.26, respectively.

As a nonvolatile memory device, no electrical power is needed to maintain a certain resistance state. Therefore, it is essential to measure the retention of HRS and LRS of the device. The retention measurement of ND based ReRAM is shown in Fig. 6(d). The output was read out at a voltage of −0.8 V. There is not much deviation in the resistance state throughout the measurements confirms the nonvolatile nature of ND memristor. Generally, for all the devices, we observed an ON-OFF ratio of 102 to 104. And the predicted retention of our device for ten years is shown in supporting data(S5). Cycling measurements have been done in a sequence of write- read-erase-read with voltage pulses of −3 V, 0.5 V, 4 V and 0.5 V respectively, with a frequency of 5 Hz as shown in Fig. 6(e). This measurement has been done for understanding the reliability and repeatability of the device. The extended picture of the cycling measurement is shown in supporting data(S6). From these figures, It is observed that the variation in the current levels is not significant. This ensures that the switching between the states (ON/OFF) is controllable, reversible and reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conduction mechanism of the nanodiamond RE-RAM device
ce:section.ce:para:
It is important to understand the origin of the resistive switching (RS) mechanism in ND based devices. There are three possibilities which are likely to occur, which is responsible for the RS mechanism in ND. The first model is based on the formation and rupture of metallic filament through the bulk of the ND film [48]. The formation of the filament due to the dissolution of the electrode under an applied bias condition leads to LRS and disruption of the filament under opposite bias voltage leads to HRS. It is worth noting that Ag was used as the top electrode and FTO acts as the bottom electrode. The voltage was always applied through the Ag electrodes, with FTO being grounded throughout the measurements. Generally, the resistive switching in devices with Ag as one of the electrodes occurs due to the migration of Ag+ ions through the active media and forming a conduction filament [48,49]. In our case, the resistive switching happens when the silver electrode is at negative polarity. That means the metallic filamentary conductions is unlikely to occur in nanodiamond film. The second model is based on an interface type RS mechanism in which the current flows through the film is determined by the barrier height between the film and the electrodes [48]. The interface barrier height can be modulated by the applied voltage bias which resulted in two resistance states. In our case, the band diagram of the device before and after thermal equilibrium under zero applied voltage is shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). The workfunction of FTO (ϕ=5.0 eV) [50] and silver (ϕ=4.35 eV) [51] has taken from literature. To achieve thermal equilibrium, electrons will flow from the metal into the lower energy states in the semiconductor results band bend downwards near the two junctions: Ag/ND junction as well as FTO/ND junction, which makes the surface of the semiconductor more n-type. It is clear from Fig. 7(b) that, the electrons in both the electrodes will see a barrier (
φ B
=
(
φ M
− χ
)
> 0
φ B
is the barrier height,
φ M
is the work function of the electrodes and χ is the electron affinity of the semiconductor) while trying to move towards the conduction band of the semiconductor. Since both the junction are of Schottky contact the respective barrier height has been calculated using the band diagram shown in 5(d) and the obtained values are 0.81 eV and 1.46 for Ag/ND and FTO/ND junctions respectively.

From the switching data (Fig 6(b)), it is observed that the SET process is happening when Ag is at negative polarity. Under the negative bias with respect to the top electrode (Ag), the Fermi level(EF) of the Ag will go up, and the fermi level of the FTO will go down, results in the reduction of the barrier height. As a result, electrons can move to the conduction band of the ND, followed by the downhill motion through ND/FTO barrier. On ideally speaking the device current should gradually start to shoot up from 0.8 V onwards similar to Schottky diode. But, in the real scenario the device current increase abruptly at the SET voltage and at the voltage below SET voltage the current follows a power law, it is an indication of defect oriented mechanism in the device. And it is to be noted that, the shift in the SET voltage can be due to the collective effect of the ND since we have calculated the barrier height using only a single ND obtained from STM.

To examine whether the conduction mechanism is mediated by defects, the pre-SET current-voltage characteristics have been plotted between log(I) and log(V), as shown in Fig. 6(f). I-V curve of the LRS exhibits a linearly ohmic behaviour with a slope of 1.01. This linearity is due to the existence of the conduction filament after the SET process. But in the case of HRS, after an initial ohmic regime, the current exhibits a V2 dependence, indicative of the space charge limited conduction(SCLC) mechanism [52]. In SCLC, three different regions exist, (i)The ohmic region (I ∝ V), (ii) The Child's law region (I∝ V2), (iii) The steep current increase region (I ∝ Vn). The HRS regime in our device is in agreement with the SCLC mechanism. In region one, the slope is around 0.9; in region two, the slope is around 2.01. The transition voltage (Vtr) at which ohmic region changes to SCLC region is found to be 0.27 V. At approximately 0.8 V the SCLC regime shifts to the trap-filled limited regime, afterwards the system switches to the ON state. If it is the breakdown due to the sudden migration of a large number of electrons through the device, it cannot be a reversible process. From this analogy, we could conclude that the Schottky barrier plays an important role in RS mechanism of the ND, and the charge transport in the device is mediated by defects or migration of surface groups which is discussed in the preceding section.

Another possibility is based on the drift of oxygen functional, which is quite often observed in graphene oxide-based resistive memory devices, and it is most likely to occur in nanodiamonds [53,54]. As we understand from the XPS, FTIR and Raman data, the surface of the nanodiamonds is decorated with the carbonyl groups, epoxy groups, hydroxyl groups and many other surface states. These surface states play an important role in the electrical conductivity of the ND films [55]. This proposed model is more or like similar to the reversible filamentary RS mechanism in which the conductive path is formed by turning insulating sp3 domains into conducting sp2 bonds due to the introduction of π electrons based on the detachment of oxygen containing-groups under the action of an electric field, resulting in the LRS state [21]. However, by reversing the polarity, the oxygen ions diffuse back and re-establish HRS. It is already reported that the migrating functional groups are
C −
OH
and
C − O − C
groups rather than
C = O
groups, since they have a much smaller energy density with Ea values of 0.35 and 0.81 eV respectively [54]. The main reason behind this physical phenomenon can be the nonuniform distribution of sp3 and sp2 domains due to the presence of functional groups in ND which restrict the flow of current. When we apply a bias voltage, the high resistivity of the sp3 domains create large gradients of electrostatic potential at a local level resulting in strong local electric fields. As a result, by the action of Joule heating in the ND there is a drift of oxygen containing groups that generate changes in the conductivity. The SCLC behavior, that we have observed in the log(I)-log(V) shows the evidence of defects which are left behind during the forced migration or desorption of the epoxy groups.

In this work, we have developed the nanodiamonds of particle size less than 50nm by laser ablation of graphene-IPA dispersion. The obtained nanodiamonds show the optical properties as that of the detonation synthesized ND. Due to the presence of the carbonyl groups, the ND samples exhibit an excitation dependent emission characteristic. The local work function and bandgap are found using scanning tunnelling spectroscopy 5.2eV and 4eV, respectively. Moreover, resistive memory devices fabricated using nanodiamond films show reasonably good on-off ratios, excellent retention and cycling properties, pointing out that other than biomedical applications, nanodiamonds could be promising in high-density memory applications as well.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2020.100023.
